data:
Search results for "CS61A scheme special forms define lambda if cond let quote eval dispatch":

Title: MIT Scheme Reference - Special Forms
URL: https://groups.csail.mit.edu/mac/ftpdir/scheme-7.4/doc-html/scheme_3.html
Content: expressions in the body of the `lambda` expression are. special form: **let** *((variable init) ...) expression expression ...*: The inits are evaluated in the current environment (in some unspecified order), the variables are bound to fresh locations holding the results, the expressions are evaluated sequentially in the extended environment, and the value of the last expression is returned. special form: **letrec** *((variable init) ...) expression expression ...*: The variables are bound to fresh locations holding unassigned values, the inits are evaluated in the extended environment (in some unspecified order), each variable is assigned to the result of the corresponding init, the expressions are evaluated sequentially in the extended environment, and the value of the last expression is returned. special form+: **fluid-let** *((variable init) ...) expression expression ...*: The inits are evaluated in the current environment (in some unspecified order), the current values of the variables are saved, the results are assigned to the variables, the expressions are evaluated sequentially in the current environment, the variables are restored to their original values, and the value of the last expression is returned.

Title: Scheme - CS61A Notes
URL: https://cs61a.rouxl.es/docs/week-11/content/scheme
Content: The reason why this is taught is not only because you make an interpreter for Scheme as your final CS61A project (where you write code in Python to essentially execute scheme code in Python - it’s really cool), but also because it’s essentially a practical way of showing that you’re able to quickly pick up other languages after you get comfortable with one. (For example, Scheme doesn’t allow you to do any sort of iteration, and also lists can only be of the Linked list form, meaning that many of the programming problems are solved using recursion). When you want to call something, you just wrap it in a set of parentheses, then you supply your operators and operands in the same order as call expressions in Python. Everything in Scheme that doesn’t behave the same way as a call expression is a **special form**.

Title: CS 61A Scheme Specification
URL: https://cs61a.org/articles/scheme-spec
Content: The version of Scheme used in this course is not perfectly true to any official specification of the language, though it is perhaps closest to R5RS, with some features and terminology from other versions, both older and newer. This document and the linked built-in procedure reference are very long and are an attempt to formalize the variant of Scheme used in 61A. You should not find it necessary to read the full references of special forms and built-in procedures, but specific sections may be helpful to reference when working on Scheme assignments and the project (the relevant sections will typically be linked in the assignment itself). Some expressions are **self-evaluating**, which means they are both an expression and a value, and that it evaluates to itself. When looking up a symbol in an environment, Scheme first checks the current frame and returns the corresponding value if it exists.

Title: CS61A Proj 4 - Scheme Interpreter - Misaka's blog
URL: https://www.misaka-9982.com/2022/12/17/CS61A-Proj-4
Content: >>> expr = read_line('(+ 2 2)') >>> expr Pair('+', Pair(2, Pair(2, nil))) >>> scheme_eval(expr, create_global_frame())  4  """  # Evaluate atoms if scheme_symbolp(expr): return env.lookup(expr) elif self_evaluating(expr):  return expr  # All non-atomic expressions are lists (combinations) if not scheme_listp(expr): raise SchemeError('malformed list: {0}'.format(repl_str(expr))) first, rest = expr.first, expr.rest if scheme_symbolp(first) and first in scheme_forms.SPECIAL_FORMS: return scheme_forms.SPECIAL_FORMSfirst else:  # BEGIN PROBLEM 3 "*** YOUR CODE HERE ***" procedure = scheme_eval(first, env) args = rest.map(lambda operand: scheme_eval(operand, env)) return scheme_apply(procedure, args, env)  # END PROBLEM 3 def pair_to_list(pair): if pair is nil: return [] else: return [pair.first] + pair_to_list(pair.rest) ``` |. (define x (+ 1 2)) validate_form(expressions, 2, 2) # Checks that expressions is a list of length exactly 2  # BEGIN PROBLEM 4 "*** YOUR CODE HERE ***" value = scheme_eval(expressions.rest.first, env) env.define(signature, value)  return signature  # END PROBLEM 4 elif isinstance(signature, Pair) and scheme_symbolp(signature.first): # defining a named procedure e.g.

Title: An Introduction to Scheme and its Implementation - An Interpreter with let and lambda
URL: https://docs.scheme.org/schintro/schintro_123.html
Content: In this section, I'll present a new interpreter for a bigger subset of Scheme; it handles all of the essential special forms of Scheme, except for macro definitions. Evaluating an expression (such as a `let`) may create a new environment, and subexpressions (such as the `let` body) can simply be evaluated in the new environment by recursive calls to `eval`. The arguments are bound in a local environment (like `let` variables), and the body is interpreted in that environment. The important difference is that `eval` expects an environment argument `envt`, which represents the binding environment in which to evaluate an expression. When we evaluate an expression that doesn't change the interpretive environment, and call `eval` recursively to evaluate subexpressions, we simply pass the `envt` variable's value to the recursive calls. When we evaluate a binding construct, and evaluate subexpressions in that environment, we create a new environment and pass *that* to the recursive calls to `eval`, so the subexpressions will execute in the new enviornment instead.
